summary: Gruber reviews Apple's preview of iOS 11.3, highlighting several notable features including Business Chat for iMessage, battery performance management controls, Health Records integration, and ad-free music videos in Apple Music. He argues that iMessage is one of Apple's most underestimated assets, noting that as a standalone startup it would be worth tens of billions. He observes that the battery throttling toggle goes beyond what Apple initially promised, and takes a dig at YouTube over Apple Music's ad-free video streaming. He finds it unusual that Apple is packing so many significant features into a point release rather than saving them for iOS 12.
key_points: iMessage's value is underestimated because it's bundled within Apple's ecosystem rather than being a standalone product that needs to monetize aggressively
Apple exceeded its initial promise on battery throttling by adding a toggle to disable it, not just showing battery health information
Health Records could be transformative if widely adopted by medical professionals, consolidating patient data across providers
The volume and significance of features in iOS 11.3 is unusual for a point release, resembling a mini-WWDC announcement
money_quotes: iMessage is one of Apple's most underestimated assets.
If iMessage were a startup, with exactly the same users, usage (a.k.a. "engagement"), and features, it could be worth tens of billions of dollars.
But because it's not a startup, Apple is under no pressure to monetize the service in ways that annoy users.
Gee, I wonder what video platform that's a reference to?
This whole 11.3 announcement is interesting. Some of these features — like Business Chat — are things I might have expected they'd hold for iOS 12.
It's almost like Apple held a little mini-WWDC over the last two days via Newsroom posts.
key_insight: iMessage's enormous value is hidden in plain sight because Apple has no pressure to monetize it like a standalone messaging startup would, which paradoxically makes it a better product.
